steps.title: Find the gesture (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly sketch a loose spiral or stack of overlapping ovals on the dock — the path the chain takes as it coils. Two or three loops piled loosely on each other is plenty.

steps.title: Mark the link spacing (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, place small tick marks along your spiral path at regular intervals. Each tick is where a link will sit. Don't aim for perfect spacing — slight variation reads as real chain.

steps.title: Block one link as your reference (pencil)
steps.description: At one tick, sketch a single chain link as a small oblong oval. This is your unit — every other link will be the same size. Comparing as you go keeps the chain from growing or shrinking.

steps.title: Plan the alternating orientation (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly mark each remaining tick with a tiny line showing whether that link will sit flat (a horizontal oval) or on edge (a vertical sliver). Real chain alternates orientation — flat, on-edge, flat, on-edge.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the first link
steps.description: Switch to ink. Trace your reference link as a clean oblong oval, then draw a second smaller oval inside it for the hole. One link is done — every other link copies this.

steps.title: Ink the rest of the chain (ink)
steps.description: Working along the spiral, ink each link in turn. Where two links interlock, stop the back link's line cleanly where the front link crosses it — don't draw through the loop in front.

steps.title: Add weight to the underside (ink)
steps.description: Thicken the lower edge of each link slightly — a second short stroke just below the existing line. The added weight makes the chain feel heavy and metallic.
